source: IRS Publication 544, 'Holding Period'; IRC 1222; Rev. Rul. 66-7. Long-term requires MORE than one year (day-after-acquisition start rule): the one-year anniversary itself is short-term.

cases.description: Pub 544: bought Feb 5, sold the following Feb 5 = held exactly one year = short-term
Pub 544: sold Feb 6 (one year and a day) = long-term
One day before anniversary is short-term
Leap-year acquisition (Feb 29): anniversary clamps to Feb 28; sold on Feb 28 = anniversary = short-term
Leap-year acquisition (Feb 29): sold Mar 1 = day after clamped anniversary = long-term
Held more than two years is long-term
Crossing a leap day within the year: bought 2023-03-01 sold 2024-03-01 spans Feb 29 2024 = exactly one year = short-term
Crossing a leap day: bought 2023-03-01 sold 2024-03-02 = long-term
